52.notes:
Jeremiah - Chapter 52
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The historical appendix — a detailed account of Jerusalem's fall (paralleling 2 Kings 24:18-25:30). Zedekiah's rebellion, the siege, the breach, Zedekiah's capture and blinding, the temple burned, walls demolished, population deported. The chapter ends with a note of hope: Evil-Merodach releases Jehoiachin from prison in Babylon and gives him a seat at the king's table.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Likely a later editor/compiler (not Jeremiah himself), as
44:30 appears to be his last prophecy. This chapter closely
parallels 2 Kings 24:18-25:30.

Classification: Historical Appendix — Jerusalem's Fall and
Jehoiachin's Release
Key Themes: Prophecy fulfilled (everything Jeremiah predicted came
true), the temple destroyed, the Davidic monarchy ended (seemingly),
yet Jehoiachin's release — a spark of hope for David's line

Historical Context: This chapter was likely added by an editor to
provide the FULFILLMENT record: everything Jeremiah prophesied
actually happened. The detailed inventory of temple furnishings
carried to Babylon (vv.17-23) emphasizes the magnitude of loss.
The deportation numbers (vv.28-30: 4,600 total over three
deportations) may count only adult males. The chapter's FINAL
note (vv.31-34) is the release of King Jehoiachin from prison
by Evil-Merodach (Amel-Marduk) of Babylon in 562 BC — 37 years
after his exile. This tiny note of royal favor preserves hope:
David's line SURVIVES. The book ends not with destruction but
with a king at a royal table — a whisper of messianic hope.

Structure:
- Zedekiah's Reign and Rebellion (vv.1-3)
- The Siege: 18 Months (vv.4-5)
- The Breach, Flight, and Capture (vv.6-11)
- The Temple Burned, Walls Demolished (vv.12-14)
- The Deportation (vv.15-16)
- Temple Furnishings Catalogued and Taken (vv.17-23)
- Leaders Executed at Riblah (vv.24-27)
- Deportation Numbers (vv.28-30)
- Jehoiachin Released: A King at the Table (vv.31-34)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | 2 Kings     | 25      | 1                  | Parallel account
v.11             | Jeremiah    | 39      | 7                  | Zedekiah blinded (same event)
v.11             | Ezekiel     | 12      | 13                 | "I will bring him to Babylon... yet shall he not see it."
v.12             | Psalm       | 74      | 7                  | "They have cast fire into thy sanctuary."
v.13             | Lamentations | 2       | 7                  | "The Lord hath cast off his altar."
v.17-23          | 1 Kings     | 7       | 15-22              | Original temple construction (now reversed)
v.31             | 2 Kings     | 25      | 27-30              | Parallel account of Jehoiachin's release
v.31             | Matthew     | 1       | 12                 | Jehoiachin (Jechonias) in Jesus' genealogy

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/jeremiah-52/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The book ends with a king at a table — not a king on a throne,
  but a king ALIVE. David's line is not extinct. The spark survives
  the flood. From this released prisoner's lineage will come the
  King of Kings. God's promises survive even Babylonian prisons."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.12             | KJV         | "fifth month, tenth day" (KJV) / cf. 2 Kings 25:8 "seventh day" — Minor date variation between accounts. Both refer to the same event: Nebuzaradan's arrival and the burning of Jerusalem in August 586 BC.
v.28-30          | KJV         | "4,600" total deportees — Much smaller than "10,000" in 2 Kings 24:14. Likely different counting methods: Jeremiah counts adult male citizens; Kings counts broader categories.
v.31             | KJV         | "Evil-merodach" (KJV/ESV) — Babylonian "Amel-Marduk" = "man of Marduk." Son of Nebuchadnezzar, reigned 562-560 BC. Released Jehoiachin in his accession year — possibly a political gesture of new-reign amnesty.
v.34             | KJV         | "until the day of his death" (KJV/ESV) — Jehoiachin was sustained at the Babylonian court until he died. Archaeological evidence: the "Jehoiachin tablets" from Babylon list rations allocated to "Yaukin, king of Judah" — confirming this biblical detail.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Historical Appendix | Chapter 52 serves as the PROOF that Jeremiah's prophecies were fulfilled — added to validate the book's claims against Jerusalem, Zedekiah, and the temple. | 
Jehoiachin's Release | The final note of hope: David's line preserved in Babylonian exile. A king released from prison, given dignity and daily bread. The messianic line SURVIVES. | 
Temple Furnishings | The detailed inventory of bronze pillars, lavers, and implements (vv.17-23) emphasizes what was LOST — and what Ezra later partially recovered (Ezra 1:7-11). | 
Jehoiachin Tablets | Archaeological discovery: Babylonian administrative documents listing ration allocations to "Yaukin king of the land of Judah" — extrabiblical confirmation of 52:34. | KJV term

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Everything Jeremiah predicted came true (entire chapter). 40 years of
   rejected prophecy, every word fulfilled precisely. God's word is RELIABLE
   — even when it takes decades.

2. The temple burned (v.13). Not even Solomon's magnificent temple was
   permanent. Buildings, institutions, and traditions can ALL be destroyed
   if God withdraws His presence. Put your faith in GOD, not His house.

3. Jehoiachin released after 37 years (v.31). 37 YEARS in prison — then
   release. Never assume God's timing matches yours. Hope can arrive after
   DECADES of darkness.

4. The book ends with bread at a table (v.34). After 52 chapters of
   judgment, the final image is: daily bread, sustained, alive. Even the
   darkest book in the Bible ends with a note of provision. God's last word
   is never destruction — it's preservation.

5. David's line survives. From this released prisoner descends JESUS
   (Matthew 1:12). The seemingly dead dynasty produces the eternal King.
   God's promises are not defeated by prisons, exiles, or empires.

------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Jeremiah Overview:

David Guzik — Jeremiah 52 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/jeremiah-52/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
